name: Tuscany — L'Eroica & Strade Bianche
tagline: Follow white gravel roads between Chianti vineyards, hill towns and the living-room finish of an old cycling dream.
dates: Best touring windows: April–June and September–October. The permanent Eroica route is available outside the vintage event, subject to road and weather conditions. Event dates and entry rules vary annually; no 2027 date is treated as confirmed. Route and booking information verified 13 July 2026.
history: Tuscany's strade bianche are pale limestone farm roads that once connected vineyards, estates and hill towns before the modern road network. L'Eroica began in Gaiole in Chianti in 1997 as a protest against the disappearance of those roads and the increasingly commercial character of cycling. Vintage bicycles, wool jerseys and mechanical hardship were not nostalgia for its own sake: the event helped argue that the landscape and its unpaved roads were cultural assets worth preserving.

The permanent Eroica route now turns that idea into a self-guided journey through Chianti, the Crete Senesi and Val d'Orcia. Visit Tuscany publishes a full 208 km itinerary with approximately 3,400 metres of ascent, plus practical two- and three-day divisions. The route is mixed surface and physically demanding, but its villages make slow travel part of the design rather than a concession.

Professional racing later gave the same roads a different mythology through Strade Bianche, whose white sectors and steep finish into Siena's Piazza del Campo created a modern classic. The two experiences should remain distinct in the product: L'Eroica is the heritage and touring dossier; Strade Bianche is a race-spectating layer that requires its own current calendar and closure information.

guideSections.title: Ride 208 kilometres as a journey, not a checkpoint hunt

facts.label: Full route
facts.note: Mixed paved and white-gravel roads; challenging with medium technical difficulty.

facts.label: Two days
facts.note: Possible, but demanding for a first gravel trip or in hot weather.

facts.label: Three days
facts.note: The best balance of riding, villages, food and recovery.

items.title: Three-day Eroica
items.text: Use the official 66/78/64 km stages as the default plan. They leave time for repairs, lunch and the towns that make the route special.

items.title: Two-day Eroica
items.text: The published split is efficient but places a long, climbing-heavy day after the opening stage. It is not the default recommendation for mixed-level groups.

items.title: Chianti gravel loops
items.text: Visitors who do not want to move luggage can sample the permanent route from Gaiole and return by shorter local roads. Confirm any shortcut surface with a local operator.

links.label: Official Visit Tuscany permanent Eroica itinerary
Official Visit Tuscany L'Eroica event page

guideSections.title: Three ride days, one buffer and no race against lunch

items.title: Gaiole setup
items.text: Collect the bike, test tyre pressure and braking on a short white-road sector, then buy ride food before small-town shops close.

items.title: Official three-day split
items.text: Follow the Visit Tuscany stages, retaining enough daylight for punctures and navigation. Use luggage transfer if the trip is meant to feel restorative.

items.title: Siena or weather buffer
items.text: Keep a non-riding day for Siena, a winery visit or a route delay. A rain-damaged gravel descent is a good reason to move the schedule.

bullets: A gravel bike with reliable low gearing and 35–45 mm tyres is a more inclusive default than a vintage race bicycle.
Arrange luggage transfer before committing to point-to-point lodging; taxis may not accept assembled bicycles.
Confirm breakfast timing and secure bike storage at every overnight stop.
Carry tubes even when riding tubeless, plus a plug kit, pump and simple chain repair.

guideSections.title: Loose gravel, heat and water define the risk

facts.label: Suggested stay
facts.note: Supports the three-day route plus setup and weather or culture time.

facts.label: Planning band
facts.note: Editorial estimate for Zürich transport, shared midrange lodging, meals, rental and basic transfer; not a quote.

facts.label: Simplest gateway
facts.note: SWISS publishes direct service; onward bike-capable transfer to Chianti must be reserved.

bullets: Brake before loose corners and keep the bike upright through the turn; white gravel can hide deep ruts and washboard.
Avoid exposed midday riding in hot weather. Water points and shop opening hours should be attached to the route offline.
Farm and local traffic uses these roads. Hold a predictable line and slow before blind crests.
Rain changes limestone dust into slippery mud and can erode surfaces. Ask locally before starting after storms.
Do not copy official prose, maps or GPX into the product without confirming reuse rights; link the source.

whyGo: The Eroica route gives the collection a different definition of achievement: not only metres climbed, but movement through wine country, hill towns and a protected road culture. It is the strongest answer to ‘romantic gravel, food and wine, cycling heritage, slow travel’ and gives partners or mixed-interest groups reasons to share the same itinerary. The three-day official split is especially valuable for a planning cockpit because its metrics are authoritative and the pace encourages real stops.

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Florence (FLR) direct, then reserved transfer to Gaiole in Chianti
gettingThere.cost: Planning allowance CHF 180–450 return plus transfer
gettingThere.notes: SWISS publishes direct service. Flight schedule and fare checked 13 July 2026; reserve a vehicle that explicitly accepts bike luggage or use local rental.

gettingThere.route: Zürich → Milan → Florence or Siena-area rail, then road transfer
gettingThere.notes: Trenitalia permits assembled bikes on marked regional trains and limited reserved spaces on some Intercity services. Verify every segment; Chianti's final connection is the difficult part.

gettingThere.cost: Fuel, Swiss vignette and Italian tolls
gettingThere.notes: Flexible for personal bikes and point-to-point lodging, but avoid driving immediately after a long gravel day.

costs.thrifty: €700–950 for 4–5 nights with simple rooms, self-guided riding, standard gravel rental and minimal luggage transfer.
costs.midrange: €1,100–1,600 for 5 nights with character lodging, quality gravel bike, transfer support and restaurant meals.
costs.splurge: €2,500+ for premium agriturismi, private guide, daily luggage support, winery visits and top-tier equipment.
costs.notes: Editorial planning bands per person, not current quotations. Event entry, insurance, damage deposit, wine shipping and premium guiding are excluded. Recalculate live.

booking.label: Visit Tuscany — permanent Eroica route · verified 13 Jul 2026
Visit Tuscany — official L'Eroica event page · verified 13 Jul 2026
Trenitalia — official bike policy · verified 13 Jul 2026

weather: April–June and September–October balance warm days with lower heat risk, but exposed white roads can still be hot and dusty. Summer midday temperatures materially raise water needs; winter rain can make unpaved sectors slippery or eroded. Pack a light rain shell, strong sun protection and an extra layer for cool morning starts. After heavy rain, seek a local surface report before committing to remote sectors.
tips: Use the official three-day split unless the group already has evidence it can handle a 121 km gravel day after a hard opener.
Confirm tyre width, tubeless setup, lowest gear and repair kit before leaving the rental shop.
Reserve luggage transfer and bike-secure lodging together; point-to-point logistics are the trip's real constraint.
Download the route and accommodation details offline, including water and food stops with current opening hours.
Keep L'Eroica heritage touring separate from Strade Bianche race claims unless the current race calendar and closures are verified.

localRecs.name: Gaiole in Chianti
localRecs.note: The symbolic home of L'Eroica and the natural place to understand the route's preservation story.

localRecs.name: Siena
localRecs.note: A powerful pre- or post-ride culture day and the celebrated finish setting of Strade Bianche.

localRecs.name: Crete Senesi
localRecs.note: Open, sculpted clay hills where heat, wind and exposure are as important as the visual beauty.

localRecs.name: Val d'Orcia
localRecs.note: The southern landscape rewards the slower three-day itinerary with villages and long-distance views.

photos.caption: A rider on L'Eroica, where vintage style and white roads express the event's preservation mission.
Cycling through Tuscany's open agricultural landscape and hill-town road network.
